item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: The image itself could not be sourced, and the author has not been active in over a year. As such, the image is presumed not to be released under Creative Commons 3.0, and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved the idea but hated the tone used and loathed the delivery. This could use some rewriting from someone who knew how to write in a real clinical tone.

item.title: Fiction
item.content:encoded: What happens if you write in a fictional place? What about a nonexistent place (e.g., "the jungle in Kansas")?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like Death Note with vacations instead of dying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple of hopefully helpful suggestions for this one:

"Subject remained unconscious for 2 days, and awoke to tell many stories of advanced technology and a city at the bottom of the ocean. It appears the previous subject drowned while at the location." I don't think you need to say that last part, its pretty obvious by the fact the next one they tried was given a breathing apparatus.

I honestly think the test with the reference to Nazi Germany is just tacky… If you're just using it to show being killed while in the location, I would use something with less baggage.

Also nobody wanted to go see the dinosaurs?????

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All right. First, technical/style issues.

+ line-by-line
- hide text

submit the paperwork to Dr. Matthews. He will approve or deny the request to use SCP-2260

Containment procedures usually don't cite a specific individual. Usually it's a title or something else that doesn't rely on the continued availability of an individual person. What if Dr. Matthews is on vacation? Sick? Dead? etc.

any deviation from approved entries will be met with immediate termination.

This is a relic of older articles, which for whatever reason made the assumption that the Foundation was staffed entirely by immature goofs who had to be specifically warned not to prank each other with the skips. If you really want the long-winded explanation as to why that's kind of stupid, I can give it, but suffice to say "don't misuse the object" should go without saying.

it weighs 4.5 kg, or approximately 10 lbs

In-universe, they wouldn't bother to specify imperial measurement equivalents. The intended audience are scientists, who will be familiar with metric.

Extra details can be specified, such as specifying exact events that the writer wants to do, but this is not necessary.

"This is not necessary" feels weird in this context. "Additional details may be specified regarding events described in the entry, but are not required for activation" or some such is a more tone-consistent way of phrasing this.

After the information is written in SCP-2260, the subject is rendered unconscious, and will remain unconscious until the time written down is fulfilled

"until the time written down is fulfilled" strikes me as odd phrasing. "until the specified time period has elapsed" might be better

One extreme case had a subject permanently fall into a coma while inside their cell.

"In one case, [insert what happened]" would be a little more clinical and a little less prose-like.

Subjects have stated that visiting a location while awake makes the location appear distorted

I'm not sure how this works. How would they be able to do this while awake if the thing puts them into a coma every time?

subject said they had a nice time

This has almost zero informational content. What does "nice time" mean? That could be anything.

The wound was bullet shaped

So…it was a bullet wound? Why wouldn't they just say that?

Subject remained unconscious for 2 days, and awoke to tell many stories of advanced technology and a city at the bottom of the ocean.

I would hope that someone's out-of-body experience to a fictional place like this would merit more than a sentence of description.

The book was initially found in the possession of Mr. James R██████

I don't understand this form of redaction at all. Why would you leave a portion of the name uncensored? Why is the first letter of his last name okay to know, but not the rest?

SCP-2260-1 has been put in the Cryogenics Area in Sector C for further testing.

Why are they freezing this guy? That makes no sense to me.

Medical personnel were attempting to revive subject by using a defibrillator.

So, aside from what I'm pretty sure is an overly simplified quantification of bodily activity in this portion, why would a defibrillator revive this person? They were never mentioned as undergoing cardiac arrest.

residents of the location were "glitchy".

What the hell does "glitchy" mean?

Leaving aside the more nitpicky, technical issues, I'm unmoved by this conceptually. It seems little more than a wish fulfillment item with some drawbacks-for-the-sake-of-drawbacks grafted onto it (the addiction and overuse things). I don't like SCP-1230 at all, but I feel like that article was what this is trying to do, except done better.

The article is also artificially long. Addenda B and C (or rather the information about the item from those addenda that can be surmised) can be cut entirely, and replaced with a couple more lines in the description. The interview in D is cheesy and doesn't give us any real information, just a narmy freakout scene. E is entirely uninteresting minutiae except for the last part, which doesn't even describe anything.

You frequently gloss over things that might have a chance at being interesting and instead use completely non-descriptive terms ("blank", "glitchy") which don't add anything to the article, and contribute to its overall uninteresting feel. Ultimately, this is a thin concept with a rickety execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hi there! I'm the author of the article and would love feedback on how to improve it. I've been getting a lot of no-votes, so any feedback is good feedback.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Another for the "has potential, lacks delivery/tone" pot. Given the creation date, I'm not sure if anyone is around who's got permission/drive to actually polish the thing. If someone is, let me know and I'll compose a list of suggestions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you give specific examples of what to fix so that I can fix it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is… okay. It's not good, but not bad enough to downvote. The whole things is entirely off in tone, but I'm low on time, otherwise I would give you examples. This definitely needs to go through #site19 a few more times, there are too many errors for me to ignore. Good conceptually, not in execution. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Really don't get why this is in the negatives. It's awesome!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Being able to survive the sun's heat

Okay, the sun is literally too hot for solid matter or liquid to be there for more than a second. Even if you find something that could stay a solid on the sun any thermal conduction would cause the subject to die almost instantly.

Also you need to block out 99% of the sun's light to safely view it from Earth, subject should've been blinded instantly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd say this might work better if, instead of a magic notebook, this was something like the agency in Total Recall that can implant false memories of locations, but with more realism in that whatever happens in the memory comes back irl.

For the memory of a lifetime, recall, recall, recall

As it is now, I don't care about this enough to want to preserve it with an upvote, so downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm, D-3237 is an example of the addiction it causes right? If spelling it out like that is bugging people, you could just say that D-class who've used it demand access to it again. Idk, a quick clinical sentence or two that works the scip into the definition of addiction might work better than the current version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, it's just the "2 weeks after the final test" incident report that throws me on this. The rest seems alright.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So should I take out addendum C, or just take out D-3237? Or something else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the overall tone, but addendum C is preventing me from upvoting. The idea of addiction and addictive personalities needing to exercise caution or be kept away is really interesting (I myself have an addictive personality), but you kind of ruin it with how the D Class went crazy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a bit similar to some other SCP's, but I feel like it's original enough. Great job.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe that the SCP put the subject into something that wasn't normal sleep. I don't think that the SCP was supposed to just make the subject dream about the place, but do something else. It would be a pretty boring SCP if all it did was make the user dream about being in a place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I hate addendum 2260-C, but the rest of it is highly interesting. I want to see this stick, I really do.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the interview! Don't see why the doctor is shouting to stop it, instead of calling for a sedative or security, but I like it.

One question though, why'd they give the witnesses to the other guy's death amnestics? He was in a Foundation cell so who'd witness it? Guards? They've seen worse I imagine, and maybe should remember it, so they don't look away next time!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but not the delivery. The "makes you go crazy" if you use it too much aspect is detrimental, in my opinion. You haven't really explored the burning questions of where, if not the real world, this book is sending people, and if it is a simulation/dream, why their experiences seem to match reality (as shown in the accurate glimpses of the future). Put yourself in the researchers' shoes, perhaps, and ask yourself how you would go about testing to find out. Then show us those tests and their, perhaps, surprising results.

Regarding Addendum 2260-D:

I was excited when I saw that last test. The potential for infinite recursion struck me as wonderfully interesting (and reckless, in the best kind of way). But to then find out that it just kills the subject was a disappointment. I'd much rather have seen what you could describe if you had the subject survive and come out to recount their journey.

No upvote, but no downvote either. I would love to see this crop up in the forum for some more refinement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some problems with the tone, but overall I really like this SCP. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Generally it's not a good idea to write about things one might want to have. Though with the right form and technique, this is a workable idea. This makes me think of one of those "literal genie" tropes to be honest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
began to breath

The verb is breathe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very thorough job. I didn't read the entire thing, but I happened to notice this.

Subjects sent to sites existing in the past have been proven to be 100% accurate, and tests done with near future sites have been proven to be exact as well.

That reads weird, but I don't know what to do with it.

You can't tell if the subject was 100% accurate or if it was the past which was. Either way, this is cleaner: Descriptions of locations from both the past and near future have proven to be accurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *May the future generations write tales of your greatness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lol, I hope to live up to the honor you have bestowed upon me :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You have been so helpful. I hereby promote you to "honorary guy in charge of being awesome". May the future generations sing tales of your greatness.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No problem! I was worried that I'd gotten way too verbose, glad to see my endless critique was useful :)

Edit: looks like I missed a couple —

"Light headedness and temporary amnesia has also been recorded in subjects." — should be have, not has.

And "after a few weeks" should probably be a number, or black boxed, not "a few" (basically, when doing technical writing, nothing is ever estimated unless it cannot be known, eg that the subject that died of a heart attack died in the location he was visiting).

Also, and this is me being nitpicky, APA standards say to either use "they" or "the participant" (psych research doesn't call them subjects anymore, it's dehumanizing…which is exactly why the Foundation uses it), or to alternate between masculine and feminine pronouns. So make some of your subjects female perhaps?

*is glad to be putting all that undergrad writing to use*

Edit 2: lightheadedness is one word.

"…and began to start breathing faster at an expodential rate." — "began to start is redundant and "expodential" is probably not what you want. More like: "…and began to breath increasingly quickly" or "…and breathing became increasing fast".

And check the tests again, looks like a placeholder number got left in and that you've got a "book" missing its "the" (addendum A and E respectively).

Another edit: "and events seen by subjects sent into sites existing in the future have happened precisely as shown."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the kind of advice I wanted. Thank you so much. I owe you one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but the grammar and style need help. So here goes!

+ This is going to be long
- Make it go away!

Any personnel wishing to use SCP-2260 for any purpose must fill out the mandatory SCP-2260 paperwork detailing location of choice, time wishing to be spent there, and activities performed at said location and submit the paperwork to Dr. Matthews, who will transfer said paperwork over to O5-█.

Long sentence is long. Maybe say they must fill out the paperwork and transfer it, and then, in a new sentence, say what the paperwork entails? Also, just say no to O5 references, give the site or project director the authority to okay the scip's use.

The pages are wide ruled, contains 200 pages, appears to be recently purchased, and all other details of SCP-2260's appearance remain the same of any other composition book.

This sentence is all garbled, try something more like: "SCP-2260 looks as though recently purchased, contains 200 wide ruled pages, and appears the same as a non-anomalous composition book."

SCP-2260 has the ability to put subjects in a temporary coma and cause them to visualize the location specified in the writing. While they are not actually visiting the real place, the details of the location are the exact same as the actuality. On top of this, anything that happens to them while at the site will affect them in real life.

"On top of this" is really not a phrase you'd see in technical writing, stick to the basic "also", "additionally", "further" and such. I don't think I'd make that its own sentence though. I'd go more like:

"Description: SCP-2260 is a green composition book with no markings or names on the front of the book. On the first page of the book, the sentence "I went to spend the rest of my life in Hawaii" is written in pencil. SCP-2260 looks as though recently purchased, contains 200 wide ruled pages, and appears the same as a non-anomalous composition book; however, it weighs 4.5 kg, or approximately 10 lbs.

Use of SCP-2260 puts subjects in a temporary coma and causes them to visit a perfect replica of the location specified in the writing. Though the subject is not actually visiting the specified place, anything that happens to them while using SCP-2260 will affect them in real life."

It must be written in the past tense, and specify a place the writer wants to go to (ex. I went to Japan), and what length of time the writer wants to stay at said position (ex. I stayed for 2 days).

Breaking that up with the two examples, combined with the grammar, makes it read oddly. I'd go for "It must be written in the past tense, and specify where the subject wishes to go, and for how long they wish to stay (e.g. 'I went to Japan and stayed 2 days.')". (Well there's some punctuation salad for ya!)

…and the entry into the book will fade away.

It's either an entry in the book, or an entry written into the book, but I'd use SCP-2260 instead of "the book".

Subjects sent to sites existing in the past have been proven to be 100% accurate, and tests done with near future sites have been proven to be exact as well.

That reads weird, but I don't know what to do with it.

Subjects that use SCP-2260 once a week or more have been prone to hallucinogenic episodes while awake, visiting places previously entered into SCP-2260.

Places they entered into the scip, or places that were previously entered by anyone?

Subject woke up and stated that someone will interrupt the test. 8 minutes later Dr. Matthews stepped in to see how the test was doing.

Never start a sentence with a number that isn't written out, that should be "Eight minutes…"

Testing has been done on his vitals while in the comatose state. SCP-2260 keeps all brain activity normal, and appears to regulate heart beat and other vital organs. It also regenerates cell structure 10 times quicker than that of a normal human. James has been put in the Cryogenics Area in Sector C for further testing.

This probably shouldn't be four short sentences, something like this would probably work better:

"Testing has been done on his vitals while in the comatose state, SCP-2260 appears to keep all brain activity normal and to regulate heart beat and other vital organs. It also regenerates cell structure 10 times quicker than that of a normal human, James has been put in the Cryogenics Area in Sector C for further testing."

I'd also call him SCP-2260-1 or something that isn't his given name.

Addendum 2260-C:
D-████ made a personal request to re-use SCP-2260 after using it previously to go to [DATA EXPUNGED]. They were accepted the request, and another test was performed. D-████ made another request to use SCP-2260 only 2 days following the previous experiment, and the request was denied due to the short amount of time between testing. D-████ was told they could use it again in a month. After the month passed, another test was performed on D-████, who was very eager to comply with all commands issued. Upon waking from unconsciousness, D-████ immediately requested another use of SCP-2260. Once D-████ was denied, they went into a fit of rage and attacked themselves, security, and research personnel alike. They were apprehended, were told they could never use SCP-2260 again, and were sent into psychological counselling. A few weeks later, D-████ was found dead with a tongue chewed off, bloody fingernails, and scratches on the wall stating "Without that book, I have am nothing".

Your technical writing just fell apart at the seams.

"They were accepted the request"?

Maybe go for: "D-████ made another request to use SCP-2260 only 2 days following the previous experiment. This request was denied due to the short amount of time since the previous test, D-████ was, however, told they could use it again in a month."

"After the month passed" isn't very clinical, "when allowed to use SCP-2260 again"?

Upon waking from unconsciousness, D-████ immediately requested another use of SCP-2260. Once D-████ was denied, they went into a fit of rage and attacked themselves, security, and research personnel alike. They were apprehended, were told they could never use SCP-2260 again, and were sent into psychological counselling.

This is storytelling, not technical writing. I'd change it to:

"Upon waking D-████ immediately requested another use of SCP-2260, the request was denied. The subject then exhibited extreme rage, attacking themselves, security, and research personnel. D-████ was apprehended and remanded to psychological counseling. Subject is barred from further contact with SCP-2260."

I feel like I'm being too critical, so I'm gonna note that I wouldn't be bothering to type this all up if I didn't like the concept and want to see it succeed — I'd just downvote and move on with my life. And I do really like the idea that a seemingly silly little thing to take a vacation with can cause people to kill themselves. But…

"A few weeks later, D-████ was found dead with a tongue chewed off, bloody fingernails, and scratches on the wall stating "Without that book, I have am nothing".

I'm assuming the D-class chewed off his own tongue, but as it stands, somebody else could've chewed off their tongue, or made the scratches on the wall and yeah, this time I would use pronouns — "was found with his/her tongue chewed off".

Also, after that, a note about "researchers are advised to exercise caution when using the same subject for repeat experiments". Maybe "as it appears that SCP-2260 causes addiction in some subjects, researches are advised…"

While D-████ was an extreme case, other D-class subjects have made requests to re-use SCP-2260, despite how dangerous the previous test may have been.

"…despite risk of bodily harm" — the Foundation wouldn't tell them about what happened to previous subjects (it's really bad experimental design to do that, ever) and the reader already knows it's dangerous, so just go with the vague threat. That leaves open the possibility that the risk is from security, not the scip.

One D-class subject repeatedly said "Give me the book" and refused to eat, sleep, or even move. Upon another test with SCP-2260, they resumed a normal physique until 2 days had passed, in which they would not do anything until a test was performed with SCP-2260. They are currently still in psychological counselling.

I…no, just no. Ditch "upon", "resumed a normal physique" makes no sense in context, "currently still" is repetitive, and the whole thing is silly since they'd never give him his demanded test anyways. That's not an experiment, that's giving a D-class what they demand for the sake of it. I'd just drop the whole paragraph and work the fact the won't eat or sleep into the last one.

Addendum 2260-D has "there was" which should be "there were".

I'm just gonna strike out my issues here, since they're minor, and I'm sure you're as bored of this as I am!

Other Experiments:

Test on 3/4/██: Entry was written on a loose-leaf paper and stuffed inserted into the book. No effect took place.

Test on 5/██/██: Entry was written in the book, and the page was immediately torn out. Subject slipped into unconsciousness and went to specified location. Entry faded when subject awakened. No change to SCP-2260's performance was noted.

Test on 6/14/1█: A page was torn out of book, and the entry was written on the torn out page. No effect took place.

Test on 6/2█/1█: The entry was written, and immediately whited out blacked out. Words in the entry burned through the white out //ink, and were visible after 9 seconds. [White out is a brand name and as such wouldn't be used this way in technical writing. Anyways, we known he Foundtion loves their black boxes!]

Test on 7/██/██: The entry was written, and the page was carefully burned so as to not burn the book. Once the page was burned away, the entry appeared on the next page.

Test on 8/7/1█: The An entry was written without an event specified, and once subject went into became unconsciousness, a secondary subject wrote in an event. Secondary subject also fell into unconsciousness, and when both simultaneously awoke (at the same time), both they claimed to have seen the each other at the location.

A few more things: your blacking out of dates is inconsistent; I'd number the tests, and make them experiments instead of tests; there's no real need to black out D-class numbers, if anything, it makes it harder to be sure it's the same D-class being referenced.

That was really long, and I'm sorry if it came off too critical. I do really like this idea, and hope it can be cleaned up and saved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really hitting it for me, but the line about possible precognitive abilities mentioned in the 'this room ten minutes in the future' test has a lot of potential.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm up voting, but I'm on the very edge. There are several tone and grammatical errors throughout the article, and the only thing that keeps me from clicking the down vote button is that I really enjoy the concept. Please, expand upon this and fix the errors. I would love to see this succeed. And if it fails, you could always re-write it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs to be put through the #Site19 wringer a few more times, hombre. Iron out the kinks'n such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This definitely has potential. I'd love to see it with the aforementioned fixes implemented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could you tell me the syntax errors/ poor phrasing I could fix?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Subject remained unconscious for 3 days, and spontaneously suffered a heart attack. It is assumed that the subject died while at the location, killing them in real life.

I'd just like to note that the whole "if you die in your dreams you die in real life" thing is an old wives' tale with no basis in reality and makes no sense if you think about it, because there's no way anyone could possibly know what someone was dreaming about if they died in their sleep.

Also, anecdotally, I've died in dreams at least twice that I can recall, and yet here I am.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The thing that will make me downvote the article is the constant repetition of words.

You used the word "location" to a total of 26 times and the word "book" to a total of 16. This really damaged the flow of the article. As an example, on the second paragraph of the description, you wrote three lines and used the word "location" four times.

SCP-2260 has the ability to put subjects in a temporary coma and cause them to visualize the location specified in the writing. While they are not actually visiting the location, the details of the location are the exact same as the real location.

It reached a point where i had to stop reading a bit to try and visualize which "location" you were talking about. Synonymy should be your best friend when writing technical.

Other than that, it's mostly tone issues. I will highlight some.

  • "and submit the paperwork to Dr. Matthews, who will send the paperwork over to O5-█."
  • "Prolonged exposure to SCP-2260 has been shown to be very dangerous."
  • "which is far heavier than a normal composition book"

So, i will -1 until you make a revision, upon which time i might reconsider my vote and give my opinions on the overall ellaboration of the article and the idea. Good luck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to like this one, but there's just too many little missteps in tone , especially in the addenda after the test logs. The big one that really bothered me is telling us that its dangerous. Its an SCP. Even Pinwheels can be assumed to be harmful with long term or repeated exposure if they're SCPs. Explain this thing's particular flavor of danger instead.

No vote for now, but with some tinkering I'd upvote.

Edit:Given said tinkering has been successfully accomplished, upvoted. Looks like this one might make a comeback. Congrats if it makes it, that doesn't happen often.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The base idea is fair, but not particularly original. Perhaps that could be overcome with some improvements to the storytelling, but the first order of business here is proofreading. I don't see spelling issues, but currently, this has a few too many syntax errors and/or poor phrasing that should have been caught before posting. Never be afraid to ask for advice before an article up - it will almost always improve both your work and the response you get from the community.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm no-voting until you get rid of that link to SCP-990, I really don't think the Foundation would want to cross-test with a Keter class entity. Also, mentioning SCP-990 feels like you're just trying to rake in the upvotes.

At the moment it seems like an interesting idea, your technical writing is alright, and the flow of the article is good. Good job.
